speaker: interviewer
text: You are, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three. Today is May 31, 1995. This is an interview with Livia Bitton-Jackson. We were talking about your brother Bubbe being sent back to Budapest--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --after the German invasion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then in the evening, Mr. Kardosz his name, a neighbor whose son was also studying in Budapest, ran over. And he had heard the terrible rumors about Jews being arrested, that the Germans had occupied Budapest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was in a panic. And he asked my father, have you heard anything from Bubbe? And my father admitted that Bubbe was home. And we heard about the occupation. And my father sent him back, because my parents didn't believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mr. Kardosz said, I'm leaving. I'm going tomorrow morning to bring my son home. And he ran away. And he left the next morning for Budapest, and he never returned. Neither he nor his son ever returned.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we knew [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that we were in serious trouble. And during the night of the next day, my brother arrived again. He managed to come home. But that day, I will never forget my parents, the tension.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've never seen my mother break down and cry. She's a-- was a very stoic, very strong woman. And it was a horrible, horrible day. And then late at night, again, my brother arrived just like the two days before during the night. The reason he managed to get home without being detected is that he was tall and blond and didn't look Jewish. And he just evaded the military police, which both the Germans and the Hungarian military stepped up their-- their vigilance. And they would then participate in taking Jews off the trains, from the streets, from street cars, from everywhere. It wasn't yet the systematic deportation. But there was this on-site arrests. And these people who were arrested were put on-- on trains and were taken away. So, the next--

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then very shortly after this-- so you asked me about the occupation. So for me, you see, the German occupation had this dramatic memory. But then very quickly things happened. Shortly afterwards, we were notified we had to paint large yellow star on the house. And we had to wear a yellow star. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm sorry. I'm sorry. I'm sorry. The yellow star preceded this. We were already wearing the yellow star before the German occupation. But at this point, very quickly, let's say, we were ordered to hand over all our valuables. We had to carry them to the main square and was taken from us. Then we were ordered to hand over all vehicles. Now, people had only bicycles. That was the only vehicle. And I had gotten a new bicycle for my 13th birthday, which I hadn't driven. Because the winter is very long in that part of Czechoslovakia. And so snow and ice was on the ground until practically the middle or the end of March. And I didn't have a chance to ride my bicycle. So I was heartbroken that I had to hand over my bicycle. We had to take our bicycles to the city hall square and hand it over. And very quickly afterwards, we were ordered to pack 50 kilogram of our-- of our belongings. And then we were shipped to another town. It happens a smaller town than ours, some 14 kilometers away. And there but it was what was called a ghetto was set up. And this was the synagogue yard. You know, each small town or probably even the large towns in Europe had like the Jewish court, which contains a synagogue and the Jewish slaughter house and the school and the Jewish ritual bath and all of the buildings of the people who serve the community live there. et cetera. It was like a complex, like a campus. And so this area was surrounded by the military police and was made into a ghetto as they called it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the name of the town is a long name. It's Nagymagyar. Should I spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: N-A-G-Y, that's the 'gi' sound. Nagy in Hungarian means great. Magyar, Magyar means Hungarian, great Hungarian, M-A-G-Y-A-R, Nagymagyar. And we were brought here. We were put in to a home of one of the people who lived in this area. Many of the towns were concentrated here, so we were several thousand people in this small area. And we lived with another family. This existence lasted about a month, during let's say during the month of April '44. And then during this period, my father was taken away from us. He was taken to a labor camp in Hungary. We didn't know, of course, where. All able-bodied men above 18 and below 55 were taken away. And so my father fell into that category. My-- my brother didn't make it yet. He wasn't yet 18, so he wasn't taken away. So he stayed with us, my mother, myself, and my aunt, my mother's older sister. We were together. We lived with another family in the small house. In every apartment a family occupied one room. And then all kinds of things happened during this time. One of them was that they burnt all our books. We had to deliver all our books. And they made a pile of it, and they put it to the torch, books, documents, certificates, pictures, every picture. That's the reason why I don't have any pictures. You had asked me to find and display some pictures from our life before. But everything had to be handed over, and then it was put to the torch. All the men who had beards had to shave it off. And some men had ear locks, the traditional peyos. The Jewish peyos, had to be shaven off, so that we were prepare for deportation. And then from this place, we were deported to another town, a larger town, another ghetto, as they called it, which was just the synagogue itself. Everybody was crowded into the synagogue of this place. By that time, we were told we were between 4,000 to 5,000 people in the synagogue building.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you can imagine the crowding was just unbearable. All you had was you could spread a blanket and each family lived on that blanket, and next to you another family. And in the-- among these circumstances, we were for a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: During this time, many people died, I imagine that people who were prone to heart attacks, elderly, and probably the fear and the crowding. There were many deaths. This is the first time that I sort of experienced close hand death, because people died. And then when the trains came, when the news reached us that the cattle cars came and we will be taken away from here, people were happy. Because we thought that this was unbearable and-- and anything else would be better. So we-- a week later we were loaded onto trains. It wasn't a regular passenger train. It was cattle cars, and-- which had a tiny little window with meshed wire on it. And the air actually we got was that a little gap was left in the door. That was chained closed, but there was a tiny gap. We were crowded in these trains. And we were taken away to we didn't know where. Even now people said, they are taking us took place in Austria called Theresienstadt or Terezin. Now, we know that Theresienstadt also a gathering point, it was a ghetto. And some people said, no, they're taking us to Auschwitz. And people in the car, in the cattle car, are discussing this. And they said, no, they're not taking us to Auschwitz. Hungarian Jews are getting special treatment, because we're very loyal citizens of Hungary. Only Polish Jews are taken to Auschwitz. Hungarian Jews are taken to Theresienstadt. And the teacher in our town, as she was looking through that gap, and she saw some of the names of the stations as we're passing. And she identified it as Polish. And she said, we are in Poland already.

speaker: interviewee
text: How can we go to Theresienstadt? And I said, oh, maybe through a roundabout way they're taking us to Theresienstadt. We're not going to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: At dawn on the fourth day-- we traveled three days and four nights actually. On dawn on the fourth day, the train stopped. The doors were open, and screaming, dogs barking. We were said quickly, get out, out, out. And there are men in striped uniform. And there were many SS men with their dogs, German shepherds that were barking ferociously. I'm very scared of dogs ever since. And they were hurrying us. And they said, get out of the wagon. And by the way, we were permitted, everybody, to take along a knapsack of the most important things.

speaker: interviewer
text: I always ask when I speak to children's groups-- and I do, in schools-- I always ask them, when you are told that you can take only one knapsack, what would you put in it? And it's interesting to know what each child would pick.

speaker: interviewee
text: I picked my favorite book that I still had, even though we had to deliver them. I had a book of poems which I had given to a Hungarian soldier before we were taken away from Nagymagyar who promised to keep it for me. Because I was afraid to take that along. And-- and my favorite doll, I still had a doll that was my favorite, and a few other items and, of course, food. Mostly foodstuffs my mother packed into all our knapsack. When we arrived-- and on the way, by the way, my mother didn't let us eat. Because she said we don't know how long that it will take before they will feed us. And we'll need that food. And so she allowed us only to nibble. Well, on arrival we had to leave everything in the train, and I was so sorry. We had a goose [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bottom. The bottom half of the goose was roasted. My mother roasted that. And I loved it. And she didn't allow us to eat it. And I remember thinking of that, oh, I wish I had eaten it. We had to leave it behind. But we were told, don't worry about your luggage. You'll get it later. In the meantime, we were shoved out of the trains and told hurry, hurry, line up. We had to line up by fives. Immediately, the men were separated from us. My brother was taken away. He was turned to line up on the other side of the tracks. He wanted to say goodbye to us. And as he turned, somebody shoved him, one of the SS men shoved him an he fell. And he was wearing my father's gray hat. My father had a very elegant gray velour hat, which is not on the picture. Because in the ghetto, he didn't want to wear it. He didn't want to attract attention. It was a very beautiful gray velour hat. And my brother was a young boy. And, of course, he wasn't wearing hats yet. But he loved that hat, so he traveled in that hat to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he's wearing this hat. And as they shoved him, he fell. And the hat tumbled.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was rain earlier, so there was a mud puddle. And the hat fell into the mud puddle. And he also fell into it. And that's the last I saw of him. And we were told to just move on, on. And many of the SS had whips. And they were swinging it in the air. And some of the whips hit us. And so we were marching quickly. And then people were there, old people, sick people. There were women with their little children. There were babies, and the babies were crying. And the most vivid memory I have is that there was a young woman from our hometown who had two little children hanging onto her skirt. And one of them she was carrying, a baby. And she suddenly remembered that she forgot the diapers in the wagon. And she turned back, wanting to run. And they stopped her. And she said, but I forgot the diapers in the wagon. And they said, never mind, just march. And then there was a striped uniform man. And later, we knew that those were Jewish inmates. And he said to her, lady, where you are going, you won't need any diapers. And she looked. And her beautiful, big brown eyes opened wide. Her name was Heidi Longer. And she looked in panic, uncomprehending. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew that something horrible is going on here. I just understood that this is horrible. Where she's going, she won't need any diapers. What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, they kept chasing us, run, run, walk. So we were going and running towards a point among the tracks. And there stood, which now I know and later we knew, his name was Doctor Mengele. He was a doctor and SS man who was doing the selection. He was sending the elderly and the sickly-- and by the way, the elderly meant everyone above 55-- and the mothers with their children to the left. We didn't know where they are going. And then the people who were above 16, age 16, up to the age, let's say, of 55 or 50-- I mean, it depended whether you were young and able-bodied and strong-looking-- were sent to the right. And my friend, one of-- this Mrs. Longer and my friend Bobbi with her mother and another friend with her mother, were all sent to the left. So my mother and my aunt and I were walking locked in arms by-- by our arms. And we're marching quickly. So automatically, we were already heading towards the left when Mengele stopped me with his stick and said, you, come here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came. And he said, are you Jewish? And I was very tall for my age. I think that's what he noticed me. And also, I had thick, long blonde braids.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the typical Aryan girl, you know, the German ideal of the pure race. So that's why he asked me if I was Jewish. And I said, yes, of course, I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, and that, is that your mother? Because my mother looked at him. And she looks probably somewhat similar to me-- she looked. I still think she's alive. And he said, come back. I said, yes, this is my mother. And he says, come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that point my aunt, who wanted to join them, wasn't allowed. She was sent to the left. And then she started to cry and said, Laura, please don't leave me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother said, officer, let me go with my sister. She needs me. And he said, no, you go with your daughter. She needs you more. So it is such an insane thing to remember this, that Mengele, who was sending thousands and thousands and probably tens of thousands to-- to their death stopped and took time out to save my life at that moment, simply because I was blonde. And I had blonde braids. And I had blue eyes. And maybe I looked like the Aryan ideal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at this point, my aunt became hysterical as they were shoving her towards the left. And I said to her, Aunt Serena, I'll never see you again. And I really don't know why I said that. Because we didn't know that the left line led to the gas chambers. We didn't know. I just knew that something horrible is going on here. And if I'm separated from her, I'll never see her again. And of course in truth, in probably less than an hour she was already not alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we were marching off to the right, Mengele said to me, how old are you? And I said, I'm 13. And he said, from now on, remember you are 16. So I knew that 16 meant that I was allowed to stay among the grownups. And I knew that probably there will be other selections when I will have to lie about my age.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we were marching towards the camp Auschwitz. We were marching for a long time. And then we came to that famous gate. By now, most people know it, metal-- black metal gate and above it the words, [NON-ENGLISH], which in English means work makes you free.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what they meant by it. But my mother said, you see? It means that we will work. And we will get proper treatment. I don't know what they mean by free, but we'll be treated properly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we arrived into the camp, the gates opened. I don't know to how much detail I can go at this point, so please you tell me. Because we were taken to the showers. There were other things I would have mentioned along the way. And just, perhaps, I'll mention that the camp was surrounded by high wire. Later, we found out that the wire was electrified. And so these were the electrified fences that many people later on who decided to commit suicide would just walk to the fence and touch it. And instantly, they were killed, electrocuted. So that in Auschwitz the expression, I'm going to walk to the fence, meant, goodbye, I'm committing suicide. And many people did it, especially in the beginning. We were walking and walking and walking very fast. And we came to a long, flat building where we were driven fast, fast, fast. And then we were inside this long brick building, flat, meaning low, low ceiling. And there were SS soldiers all over. And we were told to get undressed. That was the first awful thing to do. At first, we didn't want to. Nobody moved. And then a shot was fired in there, and this-- and then the screaming. Whoever doesn't get dressed in a few seconds will be shot. So very quickly, we got undressed. And we had to drop our clothes in a pile. And then our hair was shaven. Women came in. And we have to line up in front of them. And they shaved our hair. Didn't cut its first, but immediately with a shaver, they shaved the hair. In my case, it was especially painful. Because I had this heavy braid. And they were shaving it. And while she was-- while the entire part of the hair wasn't shaven off that was held in a braid was very painful, because it was pulling, pulling. And then finally it was all shaven off, and then she did the other side. And it was amazing the change that shaving of your hair brought about. People suddenly stopped looking not only like women, but like people. The facial expression was gone. You are naked. And you have no hair. All of a sudden you are shaven.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, suddenly, we didn't recognize each other. My mother-- I recognized my mother. Because, you see, strictly Orthodox women have their hair covered. Or, they wear a wig. My mother wore a wig.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when she didn't have a wig on, she had her hair covered completely. So I already visualized her face without hair. But she didn't recognize me. And so she-- she was looking for me. And then she said, oh, Bubbe. Suddenly, she thought I was my brother, because I looked like a boy with my hair gone. From this point, we were driven into another part of this building and another. And the doors closed. And from the ceiling, water started rushing. And this was the so-called shower. Cold water rushed on us. And then from there, again, we were driven out into the third part of this building where there were grey, like, sack-like straight dresses, which didn't have any sleeves or anything else. It was just a grey piece with the holes for arms and the hole for your head. And we had to put that on. And somebody with a large paint-- with red paintbrush painted on the back a K and an L, which stood for [NON-ENGLISH], which was concentration camp. And, again, we were driven out and lined up by fives. This was the first roll call, [NON-ENGLISH]. And from this roll call, again, we were marched. And then we were marched into the camp itself. We were driven into barracks. The barracks in this place-- later, I found out that this was a part of Auschwitz called Birkenau, which was rather new. And the barracks were not built yet completely.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was-- would be a wooden structure only with doors and windows not completed. It was just like a skeleton of a-- of a structure. And so we-- we were driven there. And we were given-- every 12th woman was given a blanket, a military, an army blanket, which we had to spread. And-- and we slept on that.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this place, in-- of Birkenau, there was no water, no latrines. So we found out from the inmates in this camp whom we met there that there was a swamp in one part of the camp. And if we were very thirsty, we could go there and drink from that. And so we tried. I, at first, couldn't, because it was very smelly. And I took it to my mouth, and I spit it out. And I started to throw up. But after a while, you were terribly, terribly thirsty. We were exposed to the sun all day. We were not allowed to go into the barracks all day. From the early morning [NON-ENGLISH], roll call, till the late night [NON-ENGLISH], another roll call, you were just hanging around outside in the heat. And the heat was tremendous, tremendous. We had a very, very hot May and the beginning of June. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was like our-- our day in Birkenau or Auschwitz. In this place, we-- we met my other aunt, my mother's younger sister, Cecilia. Or, in Hungarian, we called her [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: One minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she-- we met her the first day when we arrived, because she heard that a new transport arrived. And she came looking, looking. Each time a new transport came, people always came looking, looking, asking questions. And so she kept looking, and then she met me. And I took her to my mother, who was lying on the ground. She had fallen asleep. And there was a reunion between the three of us in-- in this dusty, hot place in Auschwitz.
